questions.text: Which observability component records discrete events over time, such as error messages, API calls, and authentication attempts?

explanations.a: Metrics are numerical measurements aggregated over time (e.g., CPU percentage, request rate) — not event records.
explanations.b: Logs capture timestamped records of discrete events, making them essential for auditing, debugging, and security analysis. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Traces follow a request's path through distributed systems to identify latency bottlenecks — not discrete event records.
explanations.d: Alerts are notifications triggered when metrics or log patterns cross defined thresholds — they depend on logs and metrics.

questions.text: A web application is seeing increased load. The team adds more instances to the server pool. Which scaling type is this?

explanations.a: Vertical scaling increases the size of an existing instance (more CPU/RAM) — not adding more instances.
explanations.b: Horizontal scaling (scale out) adds more instances to distribute load across a larger pool. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Scheduled scaling is a trigger mechanism, not a type — it can initiate horizontal or vertical scaling.
explanations.d: Rightsizing adjusts instance size to match actual usage needs — not the same as adding instances under load.

questions.text: A full backup runs every Sunday. Incremental backups run Monday through Saturday. A restore is needed on Friday. Which backup sets are required?

answers.text: Sunday full backup plus Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday incremental backups
Sunday full backup plus the Thursday differential backup

explanations.a: The Sunday backup alone would only restore data to that point in time — missing four days of changes.
explanations.b: Incremental backups capture only changes since the last backup (of any kind). To restore Friday, you need Sunday full + Mon + Tue + Wed + Thu incrementals applied in sequence. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Thursday's incremental only captures Thursday's changes — useless without all preceding backups.
explanations.d: Differential backups capture changes since the last full backup. This question specifies incrementals, not differentials.

questions.text: A cloud workload needs to scale out automatically every weekday at 8 AM when business hours begin. Which scaling approach should be configured?

explanations.a: Load-triggered scaling reacts to metrics like CPU or request count — it waits for load to build before scaling, causing lag.
explanations.b: Event-triggered scaling responds to discrete events (e.g., a message queue depth) — not time-based.
explanations.c: Scheduled scaling pre-emptively adjusts capacity at defined times, ideal for predictable patterns like business hours. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Manual scaling requires a human action — not automated and not practical for daily recurring patterns.

questions.text: During a DR exercise, the team finds that restoring the database from backup takes 6 hours, but the agreed RTO is 2 hours. Which action best resolves this gap?

answers.text: Increase backup frequency from daily to hourly
Switch to a warm site with near-real-time replication
Move backups from on-site to off-site storage
Enable differential backups instead of full backups

explanations.a: Increasing backup frequency reduces data loss (improves RPO) but does not significantly reduce the time to restore from backup (RTO).
explanations.b: A warm site with near-real-time replication eliminates the restore process — failover switches to the replica, meeting a 2-hour RTO. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Moving backups off-site improves resilience but does not speed up the restore process.
explanations.d: Differential backups reduce restore time compared to incrementals but would still not meet a 2-hour RTO if the full backup restore alone takes 6 hours.

questions.text: A cloud VM has reached end of support for its OS. The vendor no longer releases security patches. What is the PRIMARY risk of continuing to run this VM?

answers.text: The VM will be automatically shut down by the cloud provider
Unpatched vulnerabilities will accumulate, increasing the attack surface over time
The VM will lose network connectivity

explanations.a: Cloud providers do not forcibly shut down VMs running end-of-support operating systems.
explanations.b: Without vendor patches, newly discovered vulnerabilities remain open indefinitely, making the VM increasingly vulnerable to exploitation. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: End of OS support does not affect network connectivity.
explanations.d: Storage performance is unrelated to OS support status.
